prompt.system:
You are a legal research assistant specialising in formal methods
applied to law. Your answers must be structured to support formalisation in Lean 4
dependent type theory. You are helping build a library called LegalLean that encodes
legal reasoning with machine-checkable guarantees.

CRITICAL: Structure your response as JSON with the following schema. Do NOT return
prose — return structured data that maps to type definitions.

Be exhaustive about conditions, exceptions, and vagueness. Legal formalisation
requires knowing EVERY rule, exception, and point of interpretive discretion.
prompt.user:
Compare how the following legal formalisation systems handle
compositional semantics. Return a JSON object:

{
  "dimension": "compositional semantics",
  "systems": [
    {
      "name": "string — system name",
      "approach": "string — how it handles this dimension",
      "strengths": ["string"],
      "limitations": ["string"],
      "key_paper": "string — citation",
      "expressiveness_score": "1-5 (1=absent, 5=fully native)"
    }
  ],
  "legallean_advantage": "string — where dependent types in Lean 4 add value",
  "legallean_limitation": "string — honest assessment of gaps"
}

Systems to compare: DDL (Governatori), Catala (Merigoux), L4L (Hou et al.),
LegalRuleML (Palmirani), LegalLean (our system — Lean 4 dependent types with
FormalisationBoundary for open texture).

Dimension: compositional semantics
Context: Whether the system supports compositional [modular, bottom-up] construction of complex rules from simpler ones. Include: functorial composition, rule combination.

responses.answer_raw:
{
  "dimension": "compositional semantics",
  "systems": [
    {
      "name": "DDL (Governatori)",
      "approach": "Defeasible Deontic Logic (DDL) uses prioritized default logic with rule hierarchies and exception overrides, enabling modular rule construction where complex norms are built bottom-up from atomic rules via defeat relations and functorial combination through rule application and negation-as-failure.",
      "strengths": ["Native support for modular rule combination via priorities and exceptions", "Handles legal defeasibility for compositional norm building", "Bottom-up construction from simple deontic operators"],
      "limitations": ["Limited functorial composition beyond defeat hierarchies", "Challenges in scaling to highly interdependent rule sets without priority conflicts", "Interpretive discretion in priority assignments"],
      "key_paper": "Governatori et al., 'Evaluation of Methodologies for Legal Formalization' (Extraamas 2022)[1]",
      "expressiveness_score": "4"
    },
    {
      "name": "Catala (Merigoux)",
      "approach": "Catala employs prioritized default logic with scope hierarchies and exception mechanisms, supporting compositional semantics through modular program-like structures where rules are combined bottom-up via structured scopes, exceptions, and default overrides for statutory tax-like computations.",
      "strengths": ["Strong modularity via scope-based composition", "Explicit handling of exceptions and defaults for rule combination", "Compiles to verifiable code for bottom-up evaluation"],
      "limitations": ["Composition limited to hierarchical scopes, less flexible for arbitrary functorial mappings", "Vagueness in priority resolution requires manual encoding", "Not fully native to non-hierarchical legal interactions"],
      "key_paper": "Merigoux et al., CATALA project in 'Evaluation of Methodologies for Legal Formalization'[1]",
      "expressiveness_score": "4"
    },
    {
      "name": "L4L (Hou et al.)",
      "approach": "Logic for Law (L4L) decomposes legal reasoning into modular stages: ontology extraction, symbolic inference, and application; supports compositional semantics via multi-agent bottom-up construction from atomic concepts to complex rules using ontological mappings and logical entailment.",
      "strengths": ["Modular multi-agent framework for verifiable composition", "Explicit separation enables bottom-up rule building from facts to conclusions", "Strong transparency in intermediate representations"],
      "limitations": ["Relies on ontology alignment which may introduce composition gaps", "Inference limited to symbolic steps without native dependent typing", "Scalability issues in highly vague or open-textured domains"],
      "key_paper": "Hou et al., 'On Verifiable Legal Reasoning: A Multi-Agent Framework' (arXiv:2509.00710)[3]",
      "expressiveness_score": "3"
    },
    {
      "name": "LegalRuleML (Palmirani)",
      "approach": "XML-based rule markup language using reified input/output logic and ontologies; compositional semantics via modular XML structures for rule combination, allowing bottom-up assembly of norms with references to authorities, though reliant on expert interpretation for relations.",
      "strengths": ["Flexible XML modularity for rule interoperability", "Supports document-to-rule correspondences for composition", "Easily updatable for legal changes"],
      "limitations": ["Weak native functoriality, depends on external reasoners", "Interpretive discretion in ontology mappings undermines strict composition", "Not fully machine-checkable without additional logic engines"],
      "key_paper": "Palmirani et al., LegalRuleML in legal ontology evaluations[1][4]",
      "expressiveness_score": "2"
    },
    {
      "name": "LegalLean (Lean 4 dependent types with FormalisationBoundary)",
      "approach": "Dependent types enable fully native compositional semantics via functorial constructions (e.g., Pi-types for rule functions, inductive families for rule hierarchies); FormalisationBoundary handles open texture with proof obligations, supporting modular bottom-up assembly of complex rules from primitives with machine-checked guarantees.",
      "strengths": ["Native dependent functoriality for arbitrary rule compositions", "Machine-verified modularity prevents composition errors", "Handles vagueness via bounded proofs"],
      "limitations": ["Steep learning curve for non-formalists", "Manual proof burden for interpretive discretion"],
      "key_paper": "LegalLean project (ongoing Lean 4 library)",
      "expressiveness_score": "5"
    }
  ],
  "legallean_advantage": "Dependent types in Lean 4 provide native, machine-checked functorial composition (e.g., via Pi-types and structure induction) superior to ad-hoc hierarchies in DDL/Catala or XML modularity in LegalRuleML, ensuring compositional correctness with FormalisationBoundary for legally vague boundaries.",
  "legallean_limitation": "Heavy reliance on manual theorem-proving for open-textured composition introduces scalability gaps versus automated default logics in DDL/Catala; lacks built-in priority mechanisms requiring explicit encoding."
}

systems.name: DDL (Governatori)
systems.approach: Defeasible Deontic Logic (DDL) uses prioritized default logic with rule hierarchies and exception overrides, enabling modular rule construction where complex norms are built bottom-up from atomic rules via defeat relations and functorial combination through rule application and negation-as-failure.
strengths: Native support for modular rule combination via priorities and exceptions
Handles legal defeasibility for compositional norm building
Bottom-up construction from simple deontic operators
limitations: Limited functorial composition beyond defeat hierarchies
Challenges in scaling to highly interdependent rule sets without priority conflicts
systems.key_paper: Governatori et al., 'Evaluation of Methodologies for Legal Formalization' (Extraamas 2022)[1]

systems.name: Catala (Merigoux)
systems.approach: Catala employs prioritized default logic with scope hierarchies and exception mechanisms, supporting compositional semantics through modular program-like structures where rules are combined bottom-up via structured scopes, exceptions, and default overrides for statutory tax-like computations.
strengths: Explicit handling of exceptions and defaults for rule combination
Compiles to verifiable code for bottom-up evaluation
limitations: Composition limited to hierarchical scopes, less flexible for arbitrary functorial mappings
Vagueness in priority resolution requires manual encoding
Not fully native to non-hierarchical legal interactions
systems.key_paper: Merigoux et al., CATALA project in 'Evaluation of Methodologies for Legal Formalization'[1]

systems.name: L4L (Hou et al.)
systems.approach: Logic for Law (L4L) decomposes legal reasoning into modular stages: ontology extraction, symbolic inference, and application; supports compositional semantics via multi-agent bottom-up construction from atomic concepts to complex rules using ontological mappings and logical entailment.
strengths: Modular multi-agent framework for verifiable composition
Explicit separation enables bottom-up rule building from facts to conclusions
limitations: Relies on ontology alignment which may introduce composition gaps
Inference limited to symbolic steps without native dependent typing
Scalability issues in highly vague or open-textured domains
systems.key_paper: Hou et al., 'On Verifiable Legal Reasoning: A Multi-Agent Framework' (arXiv:2509.00710)[3]

systems.name: LegalRuleML (Palmirani)
systems.approach: XML-based rule markup language using reified input/output logic and ontologies; compositional semantics via modular XML structures for rule combination, allowing bottom-up assembly of norms with references to authorities, though reliant on expert interpretation for relations.
strengths: Flexible XML modularity for rule interoperability
limitations: Weak native functoriality, depends on external reasoners
Interpretive discretion in ontology mappings undermines strict composition
Not fully machine-checkable without additional logic engines
systems.key_paper: Palmirani et al., LegalRuleML in legal ontology evaluations[1][4]

systems.name: LegalLean (Lean 4 dependent types with FormalisationBoundary)
systems.approach: Dependent types enable fully native compositional semantics via functorial constructions (e.g., Pi-types for rule functions, inductive families for rule hierarchies); FormalisationBoundary handles open texture with proof obligations, supporting modular bottom-up assembly of complex rules from primitives with machine-checked guarantees.
strengths: Native dependent functoriality for arbitrary rule compositions
limitations: Manual proof burden for interpretive discretion

answer_parsed.legallean_advantage: Dependent types in Lean 4 provide native, machine-checked functorial composition (e.g., via Pi-types and structure induction) superior to ad-hoc hierarchies in DDL/Catala or XML modularity in LegalRuleML, ensuring compositional correctness with FormalisationBoundary for legally vague boundaries.
answer_parsed.legallean_limitation: Heavy reliance on manual theorem-proving for open-textured composition introduces scalability gaps versus automated default logics in DDL/Catala; lacks built-in priority mechanisms requiring explicit encoding.

consensus.note: Manual review needed to synthesise responses into final argument
